step.title: Step 3
step.actionDisplay: LEFT -> LEFT -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> DOWN -> DOWN -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RESET

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 14 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions (first 10): LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
The game is over.
Current state: step 52, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 51 actions; a strong score needs about 19 or fewer. You are 2.7x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 51 actions returned the board to a state already seen 51 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 51 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The game is over (GAME_OVER state). The level reset back to the initial state. The issue is that my BFS path was wrong - the game stopped early after 14 actions because it hit a game over condition.
Looking at the grid again more carefully, I think the problem is that my grid parsing might be wrong. The logical grid I built shows the start at (0,0) which is 'b' (blue), but the path needs to go through walkable cells.
Wait - looking at the grid:
```
  0: bgWBWgWgWBB
  1: BBgBgBBBgBB
  2: BBWBWgWgWgW
  ...
```
The start is at (0,0) which is 'b'. From there, RIGHT goes to (0,1)='g', RIGHT again to (0,2)='W'. But (0,3)='B' is a wall. So the first move RIGHT,RIGHT gets to (0,2).
But wait - the game said GAME_OVER after 14 actions of the second path. Let me think about what happened.
Actually, I think the issue is that the grid I'm building doesn't match the actual walkable cells. The game might have different rules than I assumed. Let me look at this differently.
Looking at the image more carefully - the blue square has a small black dot inside it. This might be the player character, and the blue square is the starting cell. The player moves within the grid.
Let me reconsider: maybe the grid cells aren't 3x3 blocks. Maybe each individual pixel is a cell, and the player moves one pixel at a time. But that would make the grid 64x64 which is too large.
Actually wait - let me re-examine. The blue object has 8 pixels (not 9), and its boundary is [[15, 15], [15, 17], [16, 16], [17, 17], [17, 15]]. This is a 3x3 area with the center missing (or the center is the player).
Let me reconsider the grid. Maybe the "cells" are actually smaller - like 2x2 or individual pixels. Or maybe the movement is on a finer grid.
Actually, looking at the first BFS result more carefully: the path was 36 steps long, but it stopped at (2,8) instead of reaching (10,10). This suggests the BFS found a path but it wasn't executed correctly, or the grid changed during execution.
Wait - the second attempt: from (2,8) to (10,10), the path was 26 steps, but only 14 were executed before game over. This means the player hit an obstacle or fell off the path at step 14.
Let me look at the second path: LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT
Starting from (2,8):
- LEFT -> (2,7) = 'g' ✓
- LEFT -> (2,6) = 'W' ✓
- DOWN -> (3,6) = 'g' ✓
- DOWN -> (4,6) = 'W' ✓
- DOWN -> (5,6) = 'g' ✓
- DOWN -> (6,6) = 'g' ✓
- LEFT -> (6,5) = 'g' ✓
- LEFT -> (6,4) = 'W' ✓
- LEFT -> (6,3) = 'g' ✓
- LEFT -> (6,2) = 'W' ✓
- DOWN -> (7,2) = 'g' ✓
- DOWN -> (8,2) = 'W' ✓
- RIGHT -> (8,3) = 'g' ✓
- RIGHT -> (8,4) = 'W' ✓
So 14 steps should be fine... but the game said GAME_OVER. Let me check if maybe the game over happened at a different point.
Hmm, actually the result says `executed_count: 14` and `stopped_early: True, stop_reason: 'game_over'`. So after 14 actions, the game ended. But the 14th action was RIGHT, which should have been valid...
Unless the issue is that the grid is not what I think it is. Let me re-examine the grid more carefully.
Actually, I wonder if the issue is that the game resets when you make a wrong move. Maybe the game has a limited number of moves (like a timer), and using too many actions causes game over. The pink bar at the bottom might be a move counter!
Let me look at the pink bar. It's at row 63, the bottom of the 64-row grid. If it's a timer that decreases with each move, then after 50+ moves it might have run out.
So the strategy should be: find the SHORTEST path and execute it in one batch, without wasting moves.
Let me look at the grid again and find the truly shortest path. The BFS should already give the shortest path, but maybe I need to reconsider what cells are walkable.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the initial grid:
```
  0: bgWBWgWgWBB
```
At (0,0) we have 'b' (blue start). The adjacent cells are:
- (0,1) = 'g' (gray)
- (1,0) = 'B' (black) - wall
So from start, we can only go RIGHT to (0,1).
From (0,1):
- (0,0) = 'b' (visited)
- (0,2) = 'W' (white)
- (1,1) = 'B' (black) - wall
So RIGHT to (0,2).
From (0,2):
- (0,1) = 'g' (visited)
- (0,3) = 'B' (black) - wall
- (1,2) = 'g' (gray)
So DOWN to (1,2).
Hmm, but the BFS found a 36-step path. Let me check if maybe there's a shorter path I'm missing.
Actually, I think the issue is simpler. The game has a limited number of moves, and my 36-step path was too long. Then the level reset, and my second 26-step path was also too long (only 14 executed before game over).
So I need to find a much shorter path. But BFS already finds the shortest path... unless the grid representation is wrong.
Let me reconsider: maybe the grid cells are not 3x3 blocks. Maybe they're smaller. Let me check the actual pixel positions more carefully.
The blue object boundary is [[15, 15], [15, 17], [16, 16], [17, 17], [17, 15]]. This is a 3x3 area from (15,15) to (17,17), but with 8 pixels instead of 9, suggesting the center (16,16) might be different (maybe the player character).
The green object boundary is [[45, 45], [45, 47], [47, 47], [47, 45]]. This is a 3x3 area from (45,45) to (47,47).
So the logical grid spans from pixel 15 to 47, which is 33 pixels. If each cell is 3 pixels, that's 11 cells. That matches my 11x11 grid.
But wait - the game over happened after 14 moves in the second attempt. The pink bar at the bottom might indicate remaining moves. If the game starts with, say, 20 moves, then 36 + 14 = 50 moves would have been used before the reset.
Actually, let me reconsider. The game says step 52, level 1. And the game is over. But the board looks like the initial state. So maybe the game over just means "you ran out of moves" and the level resets.
For the next attempt, I need to find the shortest path and execute it all at once. The BFS found a 36-step path, but that was too long. 
Hmm, but 36 IS the shortest path on the 11x11 grid with W+g+b+N as walkable. So either:
1. The grid is wrong
2. The game has a move limit shorter than 36
3. There's a different mechanic I'm missing
Let me look at the grid pattern more carefully. Maybe there's a shortcut I'm missing, or maybe the gray cells are actually not walkable.
Wait, looking at the grid again:
```
  0: bgWBWgWgWBB
  1: BBgBgBBBgBB
  2: BBWBWgWgWgW
```
If only W is walkable (not g), then there's no path. If only g is walkable (not W), there's also no path. So both must be walkable.
But the 36-step path was too long. Let me think about whether the grid might be different...
Actually, maybe the issue is that the game DOES reset after game over, and I should just try again with a shorter path. But BFS already gives the shortest path.
Unless... the grid I'm building is wrong. Let me double-check by looking at a few specific cells.
Actually, I just realized something. The game over might have happened because the player moved into a black cell (wall). Let me check if my grid parsing is correct by looking at the actual ASCII around the player's path.
Let me try a different approach: instead of assuming 3x3 blocks, let me look at the actual pixel-level grid to see what's walkable.
Hmm, but the 64x64 grid is too large to parse pixel by pixel. Let me instead try to verify my grid by checking a few specific points along the path.
Actually, let me just try the BFS path again but this time execute it all in one batch from the start. The key insight is that the game reset, so I'm back at (0,0). Let me compute the shortest path and execute it.
But wait - the BFS already gave a 36-step path. If that's too long, I need a different approach.
Let me reconsider the problem. Maybe the grid isn't 11x11. Maybe the cells are a different size.
Let me look at the boundaries more carefully:
- Blue: rows 15-17, cols 15-17 (3x3)
- Green: rows 45-47, cols 45-47 (3x3)
- The content spans from row 15 to row 47 = 33 rows = 11 cells of 3 each
But what about the columns? The cropped output showed content from col 15 to col 47 as well. But looking at the full ASCII, there might be content beyond col 47.
Actually, let me look at the original cropped output more carefully. It showed:
```
BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBbbbgggWWWBBBWWWgggWWWgggWWWBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBB
```
The content starts at position 15 (15 B's before 'b'). And the line is 64 characters. So the content goes from col 15 to col ~47 (since there are many B's at the end).
Let me count: 15 B's + content + remaining B's = 64
Content: bbbgggWWWBBBWWWgggWWWgggWWW = 29 characters
So content goes from col 15 to col 43.
Wait, that doesn't match. Let me recount...
Actually, the cropped output I printed was from rows 15-63, cols 0-63 (the full width). The content appears to be in the middle.
Let me try a different approach. Let me look at the segmentation data to find all non-black, non-HUD objects and their positions.
Actually, I think the simplest thing to do is to just try the BFS path again. The game is over, so it should have reset. Let me execute the 36-step path from the start.
But actually, the game says "game_over: True" and "state: 'GAME_OVER'". I'm not sure if the level actually resets or if I need to do something to restart.
Let me just try executing the BFS path and see what happens. If the level has reset, it should work.
Wait, actually I should check: is the current board really the initial state? The blue boundary is [[15, 15], [15, 17], [16, 16], [17, 17], [17, 15]], which is the same as the initial state. So yes, the level has reset.
Let me try executing the BFS path. But 36 steps might be too long if there's a move limit.
Actually, let me reconsider the move limit. The first attempt used 36 actions and the game didn't end (it just didn't reach the target). Then the second attempt used 14 more actions (total 50) and the game ended. So the move limit might be around 50.
But the BFS path is 36 steps, which should be within the limit. The issue might be that the path was wrong and the player got stuck somewhere.
Let me try again with the BFS path, but this time I'll verify the grid more carefully first.
Actually, you know what, let me just try executing the BFS path from the start. If it works, great. If not, I'll debug further.
The BFS path from (0,0) to (10,10) was:
['RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'UP', 'UP', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'UP', 'UP', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'DOWN', 'DOWN']
Let me trace through this on the grid:
Start: (0,0) = b
1. RIGHT -> (0,1) = g
2. RIGHT -> (0,2) = W
3. DOWN -> (1,2) = g
4. DOWN -> (2,2) = W
5. DOWN -> (3,2) = g
6. DOWN -> (4,2) = W
7. RIGHT -> (4,3) = g
8. RIGHT -> (4,4) = W
9. UP -> (3,4) = g
10. UP -> (2,4) = W
11. RIGHT -> (2,5) = g
12. RIGHT -> (2,6) = W
13. DOWN -> (3,6) = g
14. DOWN -> (4,6) = W
15. DOWN -> (5,6) = g
16. DOWN -> (6,6) = g
17. LEFT -> (6,5) = g
18. LEFT -> (6,4) = W
19. LEFT -> (6,3) = g
20. LEFT -> (6,2) = W
21. DOWN -> (7,2) = g
22. DOWN -> (8,2) = W
23. RIGHT -> (8,3) = g
24. RIGHT -> (8,4) = W
25. RIGHT -> (8,5) = g
26. RIGHT -> (8,6) = W
27. DOWN -> (9,6) = g
28. DOWN -> (10,6) = W
29. RIGHT -> (10,7) = g
30. RIGHT -> (10,8) = W
31. UP -> (9,8) = g
32. UP -> (8,8) = W
33. RIGHT -> (8,9) = g
34. RIGHT -> (8,10) = W
35. DOWN -> (9,10) = g
36. DOWN -> (10,10) = N ✓
This looks correct! All cells along the path are walkable (W, g, b, or N). So why did it fail before?
Oh wait, I think I see the issue. The first attempt executed all 36 actions, but the player ended up at (2,8) instead of (10,10). This means the path was NOT followed correctly. Maybe the game's movement mechanics are different from what I assumed.
Let me check: after the first 36 actions, the blue player was at logical position (2,8). Let me trace where the path should have been after 36 steps... it should be at (10,10). But the player was at (2,8).
This is very strange. Let me check if maybe the game interprets the moves differently.
Actually, wait. Let me re-read the first attempt result. After 36 actions, the blue player was at pixel (21,39), which is logical (2,8). But the path should have ended at (10,10).
Hmm, let me check: (2,8) in pixel coordinates would be row 15+2*3=21, col 15+8*3=39. Yes, that matches.
So the player moved from (0,0) to (2,8) in 36 steps. That's only 10 logical steps of movement (2 down + 8 right = 10 steps). But 36 actions were executed. This means most of the actions didn't move the player!
This suggests that the game might have a different movement mechanic. Maybe the player only moves when the destination is a certain type of cell, or maybe there's some other constraint.
Wait, or maybe the player moves one pixel at a time, not one logical cell at a time. If the player moves one pixel at a time, then 36 pixel moves would get the player from (16,16) to... let me calculate.
Actually, let me reconsider. The blue object is 3x3 pixels. If the player moves one pixel at a time, then RIGHT would move the player one pixel to the right. But the grid cells are 3x3, so the player would need to move 3 pixels to get to the next cell.
But that doesn't match either, because 36 pixel moves would only get the player 36 pixels away from the start, which is about 12 logical cells.
Hmm, let me think about this differently. Maybe the player moves one logical cell at a time, but only if the destination is walkable. If the destination is a wall, the player doesn't move.
Let me re-trace the path, checking each step:
Start: (0,0) = b
1. RIGHT -> (0,1) = g ✓
2. RIGHT -> (0,2) = W ✓
3. DOWN -> (1,2) = g ✓
4. DOWN -> (2,2) = W ✓
5. DOWN -> (3,2) = g ✓
6. DOWN -> (4,2) = W ✓
7. RIGHT -> (4,3) = g ✓
8. RIGHT -> (4,4) = W ✓
9. UP -> (3,4) = g ✓
10. UP -> (2,4) = W ✓
11. RIGHT -> (2,5) = g ✓
12. RIGHT -> (2,6) = W ✓
13. DOWN -> (3,6) = g ✓
14. DOWN -> (4,6) = W ✓
15. DOWN -> (5,6) = g ✓
16. DOWN -> (6,6) = g ✓
17. LEFT -> (6,5) = g ✓
18. LEFT -> (6,4) = W ✓
19. LEFT -> (6,3) = g ✓
20. LEFT -> (6,2) = W ✓
21. DOWN -> (7,2) = g ✓
22. DOWN -> (8,2) = W ✓
23. RIGHT -> (8,3) = g ✓
24. RIGHT -> (8,4) = W ✓
25. RIGHT -> (8,5) = g ✓
26. RIGHT -> (8,6) = W ✓
27. DOWN -> (9,6) = g ✓
28. DOWN -> (10,6) = W ✓
29. RIGHT -> (10,7) = g ✓
30. RIGHT -> (10,8) = W ✓
31. UP -> (9,8) = g ✓
32. UP -> (8,8) = W ✓
33. RIGHT -> (8,9) = g ✓
34. RIGHT -> (8,10) = W ✓
35. DOWN -> (9,10) = g ✓
36. DOWN -> (10,10) = N ✓
All 36 steps should be valid. But the player ended up at (2,8), not (10,10).
Wait, maybe I misread the result. Let me check again...
From the output: "Blue found at pixel (21,39), logical (2,8)"
Hmm, but that was after the first 36 actions. Let me check if maybe the game over happened during those 36 actions, and the player was reset partway through.
Actually, looking at the first attempt result:
```
'executed_count': 36, 'stopped_early': False
```
So all 36 actions were executed without early stopping. But the player ended up at (2,8).
This is very confusing. Let me try a different approach: let me execute a few simple moves and see where the player ends up.
Actually, I think the issue might be that the game has ALREADY RESET since the game over. The current state shows the player at the start position (blue boundary at [[15, 15], ...]). So the level has reset.
Let me try a simple test: execute RIGHT, RIGHT and see where the player ends up. This will help me understand the movement mechanics.
But actually, I should just try the full BFS path again. If the game mechanics are the same, it should either work or fail in the same way. But since the level reset, maybe there's a move limit that I need to work within.
Let me try a shorter approach: instead of BFS on the 11x11 grid, let me see if there's a more direct path.
Actually, looking at the grid again:
```
  0: bgWBWgWgWBB
  1: BBgBgBBBgBB
  2: BBWBWgWgWgW
  3: BBgBgBgBBBg
  4: BBWgWBWBBBW
  5: BBBBBBgBBBg
  6: WgWgWgWBWgW
  7: gBgBBBBBBBB
  8: WgWgWgWBWgW
  9: BBgBBBgBgBg
  10: WgWBBBWgWBN
```
The maze has a specific structure. Let me see if there's a shorter path by looking at the connections.
From (0,0), the only way out is RIGHT (to (0,1)), then RIGHT (to (0,2)), then DOWN (to (1,2)).
From (1,2), the only way is DOWN (to (2,2)).
From (2,2), we can go DOWN (to (3,2)) or RIGHT (to (2,3)=B, wall).
So the path is forced for a while: R, R, D, D, D, D (to (4,2)).
From (4,2), we can go RIGHT (to (4,3)=g) or DOWN (to (5,2)=B, wall).
So RIGHT to (4,3), then RIGHT to (4,4)=W.
From (4,4), we can go UP (to (3,4)=g) or RIGHT (to (4,5)=B, wall) or DOWN (to (5,4)=B, wall).
So UP to (3,4), then UP to (2,4)=W.
From (2,4), we can go RIGHT (to (2,5)=g) or UP (to (1,4)=g).
Hmm, let me check (1,4): from the grid, row 1 is "BBgBgBBBgBB", so (1,4) = 'g'.
From (1,4), we can go RIGHT (to (1,5)=B, wall) or UP (to (0,4)=W).
From (0,4), we can go RIGHT (to (0,5)=g).
From (0,5), we can go RIGHT (to (0,6)=W) or DOWN (to (1,5)=B, wall).
From (0,6), we can go RIGHT (to (0,7)=g).
From (0,7), we can go RIGHT (to (0,8)=W) or DOWN (to (1,7)=g).
Hmm, this is getting complex. Let me just trust the BFS and try again.
Actually, I just realized something. The BFS path I computed was for the grid where the start is at (0,0). But after the game over, the level reset, and the start is again at (0,0). So the same BFS path should work.
But wait - the first attempt executed 36 actions and the player ended at (2,8), not (10,10). This means either:
1. The grid is wrong
2. The movement mechanics are different
3. Something else is going on
Let me try a different approach. Let me execute just 2 actions (RIGHT, RIGHT) and see where the player ends up. This will help me verify the movement mechanics.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>

# Execute just 2 moves and check where player ends up.
action(['RIGHT', 'RIGHT'])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT": "Move player one logical cell in that direction (HYPOTHESIS - needs verification)"}, "object_taxonomy": {"b": "blue player (3x3 block)", "N": "light green target (3x3 block)", "W": "white block (3x3)", "g": "gray block (3x3)", "B": "black wall", "M": "pink HUD bar"}, "hud_map": {"bottom_row": "pink HUD/timer"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "11x11 logical grid maze. Level reset after game over. Player back at start (0,0). Previous 36-step BFS path failed - player ended at (2,8) instead of (10,10). Need to verify movement mechanics.", "goal_model": "Navigate from blue start to green target.", "recent_findings": ["36-step BFS path didn't reach target - player ended at (2,8)", "Game has move limit causing game over"], "open_questions": ["How does movement actually work?", "Why did BFS path fail?"], "current_plan": "Test movement with small probe, then find correct path.", "strategy_log": ["BFS 36 steps failed", "Need to verify grid and movement"], "failed_probes": ["BFS with W+g+b+N from (0,0) - 36 steps, ended at (2,8) not (10,10)"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.

For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 51 actions; a strong score needs about 19 or fewer. You are 2.7x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 51 actions returned the board to a state already seen 51 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 51 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The game is over (GAME_OVER state). The level reset back to the initial state. The issue is that my BFS path was wrong - the game stopped early after 14 actions because it hit a game over condition.
Looking at the grid again more carefully, I think the problem is that my grid parsing might be wrong. The logical grid I built shows the start at (0,0) which is 'b' (blue), but the path needs to go through walkable cells.
Wait - looking at the grid:
```
  0: bgWBWgWgWBB
  1: BBgBgBBBgBB
  2: BBWBWgWgWgW
  ...
```
The start is at (0,0) which is 'b'. From there, RIGHT goes to (0,1)='g', RIGHT again to (0,2)='W'. But (0,3)='B' is a wall. So the first move RIGHT,RIGHT gets to (0,2).
But wait - the game said GAME_OVER after 14 actions of the second path. Let me think about what happened.
Actually, I think the issue is that the grid I'm building doesn't match the actual walkable cells. The game might have different rules than I assumed. Let me look at this differently.
Looking at the image more carefully - the blue square has a small black dot inside it. This might be the player character, and the blue square is the starting cell. The player moves within the grid.
Let me reconsider: maybe the grid cells aren't 3x3 blocks. Maybe each individual pixel is a cell, and the player moves one pixel at a time. But that would make the grid 64x64 which is too large.
Actually wait - let me re-examine. The blue object has 8 pixels (not 9), and its boundary is [[15, 15], [15, 17], [16, 16], [17, 17], [17, 15]]. This is a 3x3 area with the center missing (or the center is the player).
Let me reconsider the grid. Maybe the "cells" are actually smaller - like 2x2 or individual pixels. Or maybe the movement is on a finer grid.
Actually, looking at the first BFS result more carefully: the path was 36 steps long, but it stopped at (2,8) instead of reaching (10,10). This suggests the BFS found a path but it wasn't executed correctly, or the grid changed during execution.
Wait - the second attempt: from (2,8) to (10,10), the path was 26 steps, but only 14 were executed before game over. This means the player hit an obstacle or fell off the path at step 14.
Let me look at the second path: LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT
Starting from (2,8):
- LEFT -> (2,7) = 'g' ✓
- LEFT -> (2,6) = 'W' ✓
- DOWN -> (3,6) = 'g' ✓
- DOWN -> (4,6) = 'W' ✓
- DOWN -> (5,6) = 'g' ✓
- DOWN -> (6,6) = 'g' ✓
- LEFT -> (6,5) = 'g' ✓
- LEFT -> (6,4) = 'W' ✓
- LEFT -> (6,3) = 'g' ✓
- LEFT -> (6,2) = 'W' ✓
- DOWN -> (7,2) = 'g' ✓
- DOWN -> (8,2) = 'W' ✓
- RIGHT -> (8,3) = 'g' ✓
- RIGHT -> (8,4) = 'W' ✓
So 14 steps should be fine... but the game said GAME_OVER. Let me check if maybe the game over happened at a different point.
Hmm, actually the result says `executed_count: 14` and `stopped_early: True, stop_reason: 'game_over'`. So after 14 actions, the game ended. But the 14th action was RIGHT, which should have been valid...
Unless the issue is that the grid is not what I think it is. Let me re-examine the grid more carefully.
Actually, I wonder if the issue is that the game resets when you make a wrong move. Maybe the game has a limited number of moves (like a timer), and using too many actions causes game over. The pink bar at the bottom might be a move counter!
Let me look at the pink bar. It's at row 63, the bottom of the 64-row grid. If it's a timer that decreases with each move, then after 50+ moves it might have run out.
So the strategy should be: find the SHORTEST path and execute it in one batch, without wasting moves.
Let me look at the grid again and find the truly shortest path. The BFS should already give the shortest path, but maybe I need to reconsider what cells are walkable.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the initial grid:
```
  0: bgWBWgWgWBB
```
At (0,0) we have 'b' (blue start). The adjacent cells are:
- (0,1) = 'g' (gray)
- (1,0) = 'B' (black) - wall
So from start, we can only go RIGHT to (0,1).
From (0,1):
- (0,0) = 'b' (visited)
- (0,2) = 'W' (white)
- (1,1) = 'B' (black) - wall
So RIGHT to (0,2).
From (0,2):
- (0,1) = 'g' (visited)
- (0,3) = 'B' (black) - wall
- (1,2) = 'g' (gray)
So DOWN to (1,2).
Hmm, but the BFS found a 36-step path. Let me check if maybe there's a shorter path I'm missing.
Actually, I think the issue is simpler. The game has a limited number of moves, and my 36-step path was too long. Then the level reset, and my second 26-step path was also too long (only 14 executed before game over).
So I need to find a much shorter path. But BFS already finds the shortest path... unless the grid representation is wrong.
Let me reconsider: maybe the grid cells are not 3x3 blocks. Maybe they're smaller. Let me check the actual pixel positions more carefully.
The blue object boundary is [[15, 15], [15, 17], [16, 16], [17, 17], [17, 15]]. This is a 3x3 area from (15,15) to (17,17), but with 8 pixels instead of 9, suggesting the center (16,16) might be different (maybe the player character).
The green object boundary is [[45, 45], [45, 47], [47, 47], [47, 45]]. This is a 3x3 area from (45,45) to (47,47).
So the logical grid spans from pixel 15 to 47, which is 33 pixels. If each cell is 3 pixels, that's 11 cells. That matches my 11x11 grid.
But wait - the game over happened after 14 moves in the second attempt. The pink bar at the bottom might indicate remaining moves. If the game starts with, say, 20 moves, then 36 + 14 = 50 moves would have been used before the reset.
Actually, let me reconsider. The game says step 52, level 1. And the game is over. But the board looks like the initial state. So maybe the game over just means "you ran out of moves" and the level resets.
For the next attempt, I need to find the shortest path and execute it all at once. The BFS found a 36-step path, but that was too long. 
Hmm, but 36 IS the shortest path on the 11x11 grid with W+g+b+N as walkable. So either:
1. The grid is wrong
2. The game has a move limit shorter than 36
3. There's a different mechanic I'm missing
Let me look at the grid pattern more carefully. Maybe there's a shortcut I'm missing, or maybe the gray cells are actually not walkable.
Wait, looking at the grid again:
```
  0: bgWBWgWgWBB
  1: BBgBgBBBgBB
  2: BBWBWgWgWgW
```
If only W is walkable (not g), then there's no path. If only g is walkable (not W), there's also no path. So both must be walkable.
But the 36-step path was too long. Let me think about whether the grid might be different...
Actually, maybe the issue is that the game DOES reset after game over, and I should just try again with a shorter path. But BFS already gives the shortest path.
Unless... the grid I'm building is wrong. Let me double-check by looking at a few specific cells.
Actually, I just realized something. The game over might have happened because the player moved into a black cell (wall). Let me check if my grid parsing is correct by looking at the actual ASCII around the player's path.
Let me try a different approach: instead of assuming 3x3 blocks, let me look at the actual pixel-level grid to see what's walkable.
Hmm, but the 64x64 grid is too large to parse pixel by pixel. Let me instead try to verify my grid by checking a few specific points along the path.
Actually, let me just try the BFS path again but this time execute it all in one batch from the start. The key insight is that the game reset, so I'm back at (0,0). Let me compute the shortest path and execute it.
But wait - the BFS already gave a 36-step path. If that's too long, I need a different approach.
Let me reconsider the problem. Maybe the grid isn't 11x11. Maybe the cells are a different size.
Let me look at the boundaries more carefully:
- Blue: rows 15-17, cols 15-17 (3x3)
- Green: rows 45-47, cols 45-47 (3x3)
- The content spans from row 15 to row 47 = 33 rows = 11 cells of 3 each
But what about the columns? The cropped output showed content from col 15 to col 47 as well. But looking at the full ASCII, there might be content beyond col 47.
Actually, let me look at the original cropped output more carefully. It showed:
```
BBBBBBBBBBBBBBBbbbgggWWWBBBWWWgggWWWgggWWWBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBB
```
The content starts at position 15 (15 B's before 'b'). And the line is 64 characters. So the content goes from col 15 to col ~47 (since there are many B's at the end).
Let me count: 15 B's + content + remaining B's = 64
Content: bbbgggWWWBBBWWWgggWWWgggWWW = 29 characters
So content goes from col 15 to col 43.
Wait, that doesn't match. Let me recount...
Actually, the cropped output I printed was from rows 15-63, cols 0-63 (the full width). The content appears to be in the middle.
Let me try a different approach. Let me look at the segmentation data to find all non-black, non-HUD objects and their positions.
Actually, I think the simplest thing to do is to just try the BFS path again. The game is over, so it should have reset. Let me execute the 36-step path from the start.
But actually, the game says "game_over: True" and "state: 'GAME_OVER'". I'm not sure if the level actually resets or if I need to do something to restart.
Let me just try executing the BFS path and see what happens. If the level has reset, it should work.
Wait, actually I should check: is the current board really the initial state? The blue boundary is [[15, 15], [15, 17], [16, 16], [17, 17], [17, 15]], which is the same as the initial state. So yes, the level has reset.
Let me try executing the BFS path. But 36 steps might be too long if there's a move limit.
Actually, let me reconsider the move limit. The first attempt used 36 actions and the game didn't end (it just didn't reach the target). Then the second attempt used 14 more actions (total 50) and the game ended. So the move limit might be around 50.
But the BFS path is 36 steps, which should be within the limit. The issue might be that the path was wrong and the player got stuck somewhere.
Let me try again with the BFS path, but this time I'll verify the grid more carefully first.
Actually, you know what, let me just try executing the BFS path from the start. If it works, great. If not, I'll debug further.
The BFS path from (0,0) to (10,10) was:
['RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'UP', 'UP', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'UP', 'UP', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'DOWN', 'DOWN']
Let me trace through this on the grid:
Start: (0,0) = b
1. RIGHT -> (0,1) = g
2. RIGHT -> (0,2) = W
3. DOWN -> (1,2) = g
4. DOWN -> (2,2) = W
5. DOWN -> (3,2) = g
6. DOWN -> (4,2) = W
7. RIGHT -> (4,3) = g
8. RIGHT -> (4,4) = W
9. UP -> (3,4) = g
10. UP -> (2,4) = W
11. RIGHT -> (2,5) = g
12. RIGHT -> (2,6) = W
13. DOWN -> (3,6) = g
14. DOWN -> (4,6) = W
15. DOWN -> (5,6) = g
16. DOWN -> (6,6) = g
17. LEFT -> (6,5) = g
18. LEFT -> (6,4) = W
19. LEFT -> (6,3) = g
20. LEFT -> (6,2) = W
21. DOWN -> (7,2) = g
22. DOWN -> (8,2) = W
23. RIGHT -> (8,3) = g
24. RIGHT -> (8,4) = W
25. RIGHT -> (8,5) = g
26. RIGHT -> (8,6) = W
27. DOWN -> (9,6) = g
28. DOWN -> (10,6) = W
29. RIGHT -> (10,7) = g
30. RIGHT -> (10,8) = W
31. UP -> (9,8) = g
32. UP -> (8,8) = W
33. RIGHT -> (8,9) = g
34. RIGHT -> (8,10) = W
35. DOWN -> (9,10) = g
36. DOWN -> (10,10) = N ✓
This looks correct! All cells along the path are walkable (W, g, b, or N). So why did it fail before?
Oh wait, I think I see the issue. The first attempt executed all 36 actions, but the player ended up at (2,8) instead of (10,10). This means the path was NOT followed correctly. Maybe the game's movement mechanics are different from what I assumed.
Let me check: after the first 36 actions, the blue player was at logical position (2,8). Let me trace where the path should have been after 36 steps... it should be at (10,10). But the player was at (2,8).
This is very strange. Let me check if maybe the game interprets the moves differently.
Actually, wait. Let me re-read the first attempt result. After 36 actions, the blue player was at pixel (21,39), which is logical (2,8). But the path should have ended at (10,10).
Hmm, let me check: (2,8) in pixel coordinates would be row 15+2*3=21, col 15+8*3=39. Yes, that matches.
So the player moved from (0,0) to (2,8) in 36 steps. That's only 10 logical steps of movement (2 down + 8 right = 10 steps). But 36 actions were executed. This means most of the actions didn't move the player!
This suggests that the game might have a different movement mechanic. Maybe the player only moves when the destination is a certain type of cell, or maybe there's some other constraint.
Wait, or maybe the player moves one pixel at a time, not one logical cell at a time. If the player moves one pixel at a time, then 36 pixel moves would get the player from (16,16) to... let me calculate.
Actually, let me reconsider. The blue object is 3x3 pixels. If the player moves one pixel at a time, then RIGHT would move the player one pixel to the right. But the grid cells are 3x3, so the player would need to move 3 pixels to get to the next cell.
But that doesn't match either, because 36 pixel moves would only get the player 36 pixels away from the start, which is about 12 logical cells.
Hmm, let me think about this differently. Maybe the player moves one logical cell at a time, but only if the destination is walkable. If the destination is a wall, the player doesn't move.
Let me re-trace the path, checking each step:
Start: (0,0) = b
1. RIGHT -> (0,1) = g ✓
2. RIGHT -> (0,2) = W ✓
3. DOWN -> (1,2) = g ✓
4. DOWN -> (2,2) = W ✓
5. DOWN -> (3,2) = g ✓
6. DOWN -> (4,2) = W ✓
7. RIGHT -> (4,3) = g ✓
8. RIGHT -> (4,4) = W ✓
9. UP -> (3,4) = g ✓
10. UP -> (2,4) = W ✓
11. RIGHT -> (2,5) = g ✓
12. RIGHT -> (2,6) = W ✓
13. DOWN -> (3,6) = g ✓
14. DOWN -> (4,6) = W ✓
15. DOWN -> (5,6) = g ✓
16. DOWN -> (6,6) = g ✓
17. LEFT -> (6,5) = g ✓
18. LEFT -> (6,4) = W ✓
19. LEFT -> (6,3) = g ✓
20. LEFT -> (6,2) = W ✓
21. DOWN -> (7,2) = g ✓
22. DOWN -> (8,2) = W ✓
23. RIGHT -> (8,3) = g ✓
24. RIGHT -> (8,4) = W ✓
25. RIGHT -> (8,5) = g ✓
26. RIGHT -> (8,6) = W ✓
27. DOWN -> (9,6) = g ✓
28. DOWN -> (10,6) = W ✓
29. RIGHT -> (10,7) = g ✓
30. RIGHT -> (10,8) = W ✓
31. UP -> (9,8) = g ✓
32. UP -> (8,8) = W ✓
33. RIGHT -> (8,9) = g ✓
34. RIGHT -> (8,10) = W ✓
35. DOWN -> (9,10) = g ✓
36. DOWN -> (10,10) = N ✓
All 36 steps should be valid. But the player ended up at (2,8), not (10,10).
Wait, maybe I misread the result. Let me check again...
From the output: "Blue found at pixel (21,39), logical (2,8)"
Hmm, but that was after the first 36 actions. Let me check if maybe the game over happened during those 36 actions, and the player was reset partway through.
Actually, looking at the first attempt result:
```
'executed_count': 36, 'stopped_early': False
```
So all 36 actions were executed without early stopping. But the player ended up at (2,8).
This is very confusing. Let me try a different approach: let me execute a few simple moves and see where the player ends up.
Actually, I think the issue might be that the game has ALREADY RESET since the game over. The current state shows the player at the start position (blue boundary at [[15, 15], ...]). So the level has reset.
Let me try a simple test: execute RIGHT, RIGHT and see where the player ends up. This will help me understand the movement mechanics.
But actually, I should just try the full BFS path again. If the game mechanics are the same, it should either work or fail in the same way. But since the level reset, maybe there's a move limit that I need to work within.
Let me try a shorter approach: instead of BFS on the 11x11 grid, let me see if there's a more direct path.
Actually, looking at the grid again:
```
  0: bgWBWgWgWBB
  1: BBgBgBBBgBB
  2: BBWBWgWgWgW
  3: BBgBgBgBBBg
  4: BBWgWBWBBBW
  5: BBBBBBgBBBg
  6: WgWgWgWBWgW
  7: gBgBBBBBBBB
  8: WgWgWgWBWgW
  9: BBgBBBgBgBg
  10: WgWBBBWgWBN
```
The maze has a specific structure. Let me see if there's a shorter path by looking at the connections.
From (0,0), the only way out is RIGHT (to (0,1)), then RIGHT (to (0,2)), then DOWN (to (1,2)).
From (1,2), the only way is DOWN (to (2,2)).
From (2,2), we can go DOWN (to (3,2)) or RIGHT (to (2,3)=B, wall).
So the path is forced for a while: R, R, D, D, D, D (to (4,2)).
From (4,2), we can go RIGHT (to (4,3)=g) or DOWN (to (5,2)=B, wall).
So RIGHT to (4,3), then RIGHT to (4,4)=W.
From (4,4), we can go UP (to (3,4)=g) or RIGHT (to (4,5)=B, wall) or DOWN (to (5,4)=B, wall).
So UP to (3,4), then UP to (2,4)=W.
From (2,4), we can go RIGHT (to (2,5)=g) or UP (to (1,4)=g).
Hmm, let me check (1,4): from the grid, row 1 is "BBgBgBBBgBB", so (1,4) = 'g'.
From (1,4), we can go RIGHT (to (1,5)=B, wall) or UP (to (0,4)=W).
From (0,4), we can go RIGHT (to (0,5)=g).
From (0,5), we can go RIGHT (to (0,6)=W) or DOWN (to (1,5)=B, wall).
From (0,6), we can go RIGHT (to (0,7)=g).
From (0,7), we can go RIGHT (to (0,8)=W) or DOWN (to (1,7)=g).
Hmm, this is getting complex. Let me just trust the BFS and try again.
Actually, I just realized something. The BFS path I computed was for the grid where the start is at (0,0). But after the game over, the level reset, and the start is again at (0,0). So the same BFS path should work.
But wait - the first attempt executed 36 actions and the player ended at (2,8), not (10,10). This means either:
1. The grid is wrong
2. The movement mechanics are different
3. Something else is going on
Let me try a different approach. Let me execute just 2 actions (RIGHT, RIGHT) and see where the player ends up. This will help me verify the movement mechanics.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# The game reset. Let me test movement mechanics with a small probe.
# Execute just 2 moves and check where player ends up.
action(['RIGHT', 'RIGHT'])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT": "Move player one logical cell in that direction (HYPOTHESIS - needs verification)"}, "object_taxonomy": {"b": "blue player (3x3 block)", "N": "light green target (3x3 block)", "W": "white block (3x3)", "g": "gray block (3x3)", "B": "black wall", "M": "pink HUD bar"}, "hud_map": {"bottom_row": "pink HUD/timer"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "11x11 logical grid maze. Level reset after game over. Player back at start (0,0). Previous 36-step BFS path failed - player ended at (2,8) instead of (10,10). Need to verify movement mechanics.", "goal_model": "Navigate from blue start to green target.", "recent_findings": ["36-step BFS path didn't reach target - player ended at (2,8)", "Game has move limit causing game over"], "open_questions": ["How does movement actually work?", "Why did BFS path fail?"], "current_plan": "Test movement with small probe, then find correct path.", "strategy_log": ["BFS 36 steps failed", "Need to verify grid and movement"], "failed_probes": ["BFS with W+g+b+N from (0,0) - 36 steps, ended at (2,8) not (10,10)"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
